topic: Globe values relabeled as dome predictions
our_argument_summary: Section 6.4 argues that the dome model systematically adopts well-established standard-physics observations and rebrands them as dome predictions. Uses four example domains: tidal periods (5 WINs), Hubble Law (WIN-047), P-wave shadow zone (WIN-064), and Roaring 40s (WIN-024). Cites code_analysis tag data showing {{CA_RELABELS}} of {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs (currently 49/70 = 70%) relabel standard physics.

holes_found.description: The parenthetical '(nearly three-quarters of all claims)' overstates the actual 70% figure. Three-quarters is 75%, and 70% is a full 5 percentage points below that. A dome defender could point out this imprecision and use it to undermine our credibility on numerical claims — especially ironic given that we repeatedly criticize the dome model for imprecise percentage claims. 70% is 'seven in ten,' not 'nearly three-quarters.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change '(nearly three-quarters of all claims)' to '(70% of all claims)' or '(seven in ten claims)'. Let the computed {{CA_RELABELS}}/{{TOTAL_WINS}} fraction speak for itself without rounding up. This maintains our standard of precision.

holes_found.description: WIN-024 (Roaring 40s) has relabels_standard=false in its code_analysis tags but appears in a section titled 'Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions.' The section does label it 'related pattern, false correlation,' but including a non-relabeling WIN in a relabeling section muddies the taxonomy. A defender could say: 'Even your own tags disagree with your own section grouping — you threw in an unrelated WIN to pad the example list.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either move the WIN-024 paragraph to a different section (it fits better in a 'non-discriminating correlations' discussion, or as an example under Section 6.6 where accuracy claims are examined), or add an explicit transition sentence like 'A related but distinct pattern is false causal attribution, where the dome claims a mechanistic connection between geographically co-located phenomena:' to make clear this is a different failure mode.

holes_found.description: The 'strategic timing' argument about tidal WINs ('registered on 2026-04-05 — hours before this critical review was launched — a batch expansion of the WIN count using well-established constants') attributes motive without evidence. We don't know when the dome author began working on V51.0 or whether the timing was coincidental. Attribution of motive is an ad hominem argument and weakens an otherwise strong factual section. A defender would say: 'You're engaging in conspiracy thinking. I was updating my model independently; that you launched your review at the same time is your scheduling, not my deception.'
holes_found.recommendation: Remove or soften the motive attribution. The factual argument is strong enough without it: 'All five were added in V51.0, registered on 2026-04-05. Adopting nineteenth-century tidal constants as new predictions adds no discriminating power to the model regardless of when they were added.' This says the same thing without impugning intent.

holes_found.description: The claim 'known since Laplace (1775) and George Darwin (1883)' is slightly imprecise. Laplace's 1775 work described tidal theory but did not isolate individual harmonic constituents by modern names. George Darwin (1883) formalized the harmonic development of tidal forces. The actual M2 period (12.42 hours = half a lunar day) was known observationally for millennia before either scientist. The attribution gives the right impression but a historian of science could nitpick it.
holes_found.recommendation: Change to 'known since at least the 18th century, with modern harmonic analysis formalized by George Darwin (1883).' This is more defensible.

holes_found.description: The Hubble Law paragraph states the dome 'has no galactic-scale mechanism — it describes a local cavity ~20,000 km across.' But the dome model explicitly claims an 'aetheric redshift' mechanism that operates at all distances (their λ_A parameter). Saying they have 'no mechanism' is slightly strawmanning — they DO claim a mechanism, it just happens to produce identical numerical results to standard cosmology because it uses the same H₀. The stronger argument is that their mechanism is numerically identical to and non-discriminating from standard Hubble's Law, not that it doesn't exist.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe from 'has no galactic-scale mechanism' to 'claims an aetheric redshift mechanism, but this produces λ_A = c/H₀ = 4,283 Mpc — numerically identical to standard cosmology's Hubble length — because it uses the standard value H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc. The dome model does not derive H₀ from dome geometry; it imports the globe-cosmology value and relabels the cause.' This is more precise and harder to rebut.

stronger_arguments.description: The section should explicitly address the strongest dome rebuttal: 'Relabeling is how paradigm shifts work. Newton relabeled Kepler's laws as gravitational consequences. Every theory re-explains known phenomena through its own mechanism.' The counter is: paradigm shifts earn their relabeling by ALSO explaining previously unexplained phenomena or making novel, discriminating predictions. The dome model's relabeling adds no explanatory power — it produces identical numerical predictions because it uses the same formulas with renamed variables. A genuine paradigm shift would predict something standard physics cannot; the relabeling WINs don't.
sources: Kuhn (1962) Structure of Scientific Revolutions — paradigm shifts re-explain known data AND resolve anomalies/make novel predictions

stronger_arguments.description: The P-wave shadow zone argument (WIN-064) is the strongest example in this section and deserves more prominence. The azimuthal symmetry argument is devastating: a wall at a fixed location cannot produce a symmetric ring. This should be highlighted as the exemplary case of why relabeling fails — the dome's proposed mechanism is geometrically incompatible with the observation, not just redundant with standard physics. Consider leading with this example rather than burying it as the third example.

The section could strengthen the quantitative dimension by noting: of the 49 WINs tagged relabels_standard, how many have derives_from_dome=true? If few or none derive their predicted values from dome geometry, the relabeling is not just redundant but genuinely parasitic — the dome CANNOT produce the values from its own parameters and MUST import standard physics values. This transforms the argument from 'they explain the same thing' to 'they can ONLY explain things by importing our numbers.'

kernel_of_truth.description: Scientific theories DO re-explain known phenomena — that's a normal part of theory development, not inherently dishonest. The dome model's approach of connecting diverse phenomena (tidal periods, geomagnetic anomalies, Schumann resonance, redshift) under a single geometric framework is structurally similar to what any unification theory attempts. The intellectual ambition to explain everything from a small set of parameters is genuinely praiseworthy in principle.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The problem isn't the ambition but the execution. Every 'prediction' uses the standard physics numerical value, not a value derived from dome geometry. A theory that explains everything by importing everyone else's numbers explains nothing. Newton's gravity didn't just re-explain Kepler's orbits — it predicted the specific form of perturbations that Kepler couldn't account for. The dome model, by contrast, has no case where its framework predicts a different numerical value than standard physics that is then confirmed by measurement. The relabeling is hollow.

text_fingerprint.section_title: 6.4 Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions

deeper_analysis: Section 6.4 is one of the more structurally important sections because 'relabeling' is the single most common pattern in the dome model (70% of WINs). The core argument is sound: the dome model systematically adopts known values and renames their causes. However, the section has three moderate vulnerabilities: (1) the 'nearly three-quarters' overstatement, which is exactly the kind of imprecision we criticize the dome for; (2) the strategic-timing motive attribution, which is an unnecessary ad hominem in an otherwise evidence-based section; and (3) the WIN-024 inclusion in a relabeling section when its tag says it's NOT a relabeling case. None of these threaten the core argument, but together they give a motivated defender three concrete 'sloppy scholarship' complaints that could be used to dismiss the whole section without engaging with the substance. The P-wave shadow zone example is the strongest argument in the section and should be more prominently featured. The tidal example is also strong but the self-contradiction angle (Section 2.4 connection) is the real killer — that the dome's local moon at 2,534 km cannot produce two-bulge tides regardless of mechanism. The transparency note about counting five WINs from one geometric failure is excellent and should be preserved.

citations_verified: Laplace (1775) — general reference to tidal theory, imprecise but directionally correct
George Darwin (1883) — harmonic analysis of tides, correct attribution
Section 2.4 cross-reference — tidal pattern self-contradiction exists and is consistent
Part 2b cross-reference — code analysis section exists with relabeling data
Oldham (1906) — cited in WIN-064 detail, correct for P-wave shadow zone discovery
citations_unchecked: H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc — standard value, not formally cited in section text
Pantheon+ compilation — cited in WIN-047 detail but not in section 6.4 prose

advocate_mode.best_defense:
Your 'relabeling' critique proves too much. By this standard, EVERY competing theory 'relabels' the same observations — that's what competing theories DO. Your review says we 'rename the mechanism' from ionospheric to aetheric, gravitational to electromagnetic, etc. But this is exactly what YOUR review's own methodology section says a good theory should do: provide an alternative mechanism that explains the same data. You can't simultaneously demand we explain the same observations as standard physics AND criticize us for explaining the same observations. If we predicted DIFFERENT values, you'd say we're refuted by data. Since we predict the SAME values through a DIFFERENT mechanism, you say we're just relabeling. There is no outcome in your framework where our model can succeed. That's unfalsifiable methodology, not unfalsifiable physics.

Furthermore, you admit 70% of our WINs 'relabel standard physics.' That means 30% DON'T. Where's your analysis of the 30% that make claims standard physics doesn't? If even a handful of those are genuine novel predictions confirmed by data, the relabeling critique — while valid for the 70% — doesn't invalidate the model.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a paragraph directly addressing the 'you can't win either way' objection. Something like: 'The problem with relabeling is not that the dome model explains known phenomena — every theory must do that. The problem is that the dome model produces identical numerical predictions BECAUSE it imports standard physics values rather than deriving them from dome geometry. A genuine alternative theory would arrive at 7.83 Hz for Schumann resonance through dome-cavity equations; instead, the dome uses the globe's spherical-cavity formula (f = c / 2πR). A genuine alternative would derive H₀ = 70 from aetheric parameters; instead, it imports the standard cosmological value. Relabeling becomes a criticism when the new label does no explanatory work — when removing the dome framework and keeping standard physics changes nothing about the predicted values. As for the 30% that don't relabel standard physics: these are addressed individually in Part 3, where the non-relabeling WINs are found to be refuted by data, self-contradicted, or unfalsifiable.'

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No contradictions between Section 6.4 and the related sections. SEC-2.4 (tidal self-contradiction) reinforces the tidal WINs argument. SEC-2b.2 (relabeling standard physics in Part 2b) covers the same topic from a code-analysis perspective, which creates potential redundancy but not inconsistency. One minor alignment issue: SEC-6.4 says 'nearly three-quarters' while the actual tag data from SEC-2b.2 would compute the precise fraction — these should use the same phrasing.

claims_checked: WIN-024 relabels_standard=false (confirmed — inconsistent with section title)
quantitative_verification.computations: Verified λ_A calculation: 299792.458/70 = 4282.749 Mpc, rounding to 4283. Verified tidal period arithmetic. Verified relabels_standard count from wins.json. Verified 70% ≠ 'nearly three-quarters' (75%).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: 'Nearly three-quarters' overstates 70% by 5 percentage points. WIN-024 appears in a relabeling section but has relabels_standard=false.
